query: current guidelines for treating sinus tracts from nonvital teeth

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Teeth -- Contraindications -- Proximity to Vital Structures
retrieved_docs.content: Proximity to the antral sinuses can be a reason for caution when planning an extraction due to the potential to create an oroantral communication (OAC). However, if a tooth is acutely symptomatic, a high risk of an OAC may not be a sufficient reason to avoid extraction. Radiographic signs that a root apex sits in the sinus include deviation of the sinus floor, a lack of continuity of the sinus floor, and long roots visually invading the antral space. A clear consent process with the patient is key in these scenarios.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Unerupted Teeth -- Contraindications -- Tooth Proximity to Vital Structures
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of unerupted maxillary teeth can lead to an oroantral communication (OAC) if the tooth is in close proximity to the floor of the maxillary sinus. Radiographic images are required for a proper evaluation of impacted tooth-sinus proximity.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Permanent Dentition -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: The maxillary posterior teeth lie near the antrum, and several complications can result from this relationship. Oro-antral communication formation is a recognized complication of extraction of maxillary posterior teeth, which requires prompt closure to prevent sinus contamination, formation of an epithelialized fistulous tract, and development of chronic sinusitis. [44] Recognition of root morphology will guide the optimal extraction technique and ensure the clinician can determine complete removal. There is a risk of displacing these fragments into the maxillary antrum or adjacent anatomical structures in cases of root fracture. [45] Extrusion of sodium hypochlorite into the maxillary antrum is a potential risk of endodontic treatment of maxillary posterior teeth. [46]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Roots -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: The increased risk of unintentional displacement of fragments into nearby spaces such as the maxillary sinus, fracture of supporting alveolar bone, or development of an oroantral communication may also preclude removal. [12] Sometimes, prosthetics replacement drives the decision to keep or remove root segments. Retaining vital root segments could preserve alveolar bone. This approach is referred to as root submergence and may be the approach of choice when considering future prosthodontic options. [12] Alternatively, root fractures occurring at the time of surgery in the setting of a vital pulp with no marked root mobilization and appropriate wound closure can often be left indefinitely to allow for healing. Lastly, always consider the patient's desires for treatment. Patients may decline treatment despite indications for removal. [9] Therefore, a patient's declination of treatment is always a contraindication to root extraction.

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Absolute dental contraindications include a hopeless dentition that is better treated with tooth removal and complete denture therapy. A hopeless dentition usually features more than two-thirds bone loss at most teeth, including canines and first molars, or significant generalized tooth mobility.

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Indications -- Wedge Procedure
retrieved_docs.content: Residual pocketing at a distal or mesial tooth surface that faces excessively thick gingival tissue Typically at the most distal tooth of the arch, adjacent to thick tissue of the maxillary tuberosity or the retromolar pad

retrieved_docs.title: Sinus Endoscopic Surgery -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Contraindications for FESS include patients who have general contraindications for general or local anesthesia.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Teeth -- Contraindications -- Proximity to Vital Structures
retrieved_docs.content: Proximity to the inferior dental nerve (IDN) is a common reason for deciding against dental extractions, primarily wisdom teeth. Radiographic signs of mandibular third molars close to the IDN include banding of the roots, loss of cortication of the IDN, and deviation of the IDN. Cone beam computerized tomography is useful in cases where the relationship between a mandibular molar and the IDN needs further investigation and visualization. The introduction of coronectomy techniques, where the coronal portion of the crown is removed to below the level of the cementoenamel junction, and the roots brought to at least 3 mm below the level of the surrounding bone, has provided an alternative treatment to total extraction of the tooth in scenarios where there is concern over IDN injury risk. [34]

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Orofacial Space Infections -- Treatment / Management -- Odontogenic Sinusitis
retrieved_docs.content: The maxillary teeth, particularly the molars and pre-molars, are closely associated with the maxillary sinus. Rarely on CT imaging or endoscopy, tooth roots and dental implants can be seen within the maxillary sinus. While this is often an incidental finding, occasionally, poor dental hygiene or a malpositioned implant can allow for the spread of infection into the maxillary sinus. In some patients with a history of oral or maxillofacial surgery, persistent oroantral fistulas can occur, serving as a conduit for opportunistic infection. [20] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Cone Beam Computed Tomography -- Clinical Significance -- Maxillary Sinus Floor Elevation
retrieved_docs.content: If the amount of bone between the ridge crest and the maxillary sinus floor is inadequate, <5 mm, an open sinus lift procedure is indicated. Preoperative CBCT or CT before open sinus lift surgery is recommended to evaluate certain factors like membrane thickness, presence of sinus septa, alveolar antral artery trajectory, and residual bone height. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Roots -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: Radiographic examination is particularly important when dealing with retained roots. With the increasing availability and popularity of cone-beam computed tomography (CBCT), it has become much easier to determine the specific depth, position, size, curvature, and proximity of remaining root fragments to nearby anatomical landmarks like the maxillary sinus and the inferior alveolar nerve. [14] This is the primary limitation of traditional two-dimensional radiographs, though they are still indicated in the initial assessment of retained roots. Not all exodontia cases require a CBCT evaluation, but it becomes valuable when the anticipated surgery increases the risk of iatrogenic damage. Pre-operative evaluation not only gives the surgeon the means to generate an appropriate diagnosis but also determines the proper course of treatment. [14] The surgeon must exercise sound clinical judgment and base their decision-making on empirical evidence and best practices when treating retained root tips. Lastly, the surgeon must decide if they have the clinical skills and armamentarium necessary to perform the procedure safely and effectively. It is never the wrong answer to refer a patient to a higher level of care or to a provider with known expertise in treating a particular condition. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Odontogenic Orofacial Space Infections -- Treatment / Management -- Odontogenic Sinusitis
retrieved_docs.content: Dental infections (or normal oral flora) can spread superiorly along the maxilla, causing sinusitis. Sinusitis of odontogenic origin is common, accounting for nearly 40% of all acute bacterial maxillary sinusitis. [7] [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Sinusitis -- Treatment / Management -- Medical Management
retrieved_docs.content: Antibiotics can be given for an extended period of three weeks. However, there is no consensus on their routine use in chronic sinusitis, nor is their consensus on antibiotic selection.

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontics, Pulp Space Anatomy and Access Cavity of Anterior Teeth -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The UltraAC is indicated for anterior teeth with intact crowns and minimal structural loss, particularly when preoperative imaging confirms a single straight canal. These cases often benefit from the use of high magnification or guided access methods to maintain precision while preserving as much tooth structure as possible.

retrieved_docs.title: Oral Surgery, Extraction of Unerupted Teeth -- Contraindications -- Cardiovascular Diseases
retrieved_docs.content: Patients who have a high risk of developing infective endocarditis or have had it in the past may need antibiotic prophylaxis. Consultation with a cardiologist or primary care provider is recommended. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontics, Pulp Space Anatomy and Access Cavity of Anterior Teeth -- Technique -- Access Cavity Basic Principles
retrieved_docs.content: Attention must also focus on replacing defective restorations. Faulty fillings compromise the coronal seal, limiting the effectiveness of endodontic therapy; replacement ensures proper closure of the access cavity. [47] [50] Finally, unsupported tooth tissue should be removed, as such remnants may compromise the seal and produce inaccuracies in determining the true length of the tooth. [47] [51] Careful adherence to these principles promotes predictable canal access, effective debridement, and long-term treatment success.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1807
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on Nose encompass the specific intervention of control of epistaxis, which is classified as a type of nasal procedure. The control of epistaxis is performed directly on the nose (nos), indicating that the nose is the precise anatomical site targeted by this bleeding‑control technique.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1981
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on Nose are medical interventions that address nasal disorders (اِضطِراب أنفي). Within this category, control of epistaxis is identified as a specific type of Procedure on Nose, indicating that managing nosebleeds is a subset of nasal procedural care. This relationship highlights that interventions aimed at controlling epistaxis are directly linked to the broader management of nasal disorders, emphasizing the role of targeted procedural treatment in the evaluation and care of patients with nasal pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2576
retrieved_docs.content: Control of epistaxis is a medical procedure whose direct site of action is the nose (nos).

retrieved_docs.content: or discectomy. In addition, excision is employed in therapeutic procedures of the nose, mouth, and pharynx and is utilized in plastic surgery of the nose, illustrating its wide applicability across multiple organ systems within the hierarchical clinical‑action framework guiding diagnostic and therapeutic decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 250
retrieved_docs.content: The extraction of a single tooth is a direct dental procedure performed on the zuby (teeth), involving the removal of one tooth from the oral cavity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 4 -- Community 2698
retrieved_docs.content: Control of epistaxis is a therapeutic procedure that is performed directly on the nose (nos) and serves as the clinical focus of the Arabic term الرُعاف, which denotes a nosebleed.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 72
retrieved_docs.content: The category “diseases of mouth; excluding dental” is classified as a subcategory of “اِضطِراب الغدة اللُّعابِيَّة” (salivary gland disorder), indicating that non‑dental oral diseases are considered part of the broader spectrum of salivary gland pathology. This relationship highlights the importance of evaluating salivary gland function when assessing oral conditions that are not of dental origin, thereby guiding more comprehensive diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1929
retrieved_docs.content: Nasal endoscopy with maxillary antrostomy is classified as a diagnostic procedure involving the nose, mouth, and pharynx, providing clinicians with direct endoscopic visualization and access to the maxillary sinus for assessment of sinonasal pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 259
retrieved_docs.content: and to the neurosurgical and spinal explorations. Additional Excision applications include therapeutic procedures involving the nose, mouth and pharynx and are related to plastic procedures on the nose. Together, Exploration provides an umbrella framework for targeted surgical investigations, while Excision represents the core operative technique that enables tissue removal, obstruction relief, diagnostic assessment and neural decompression, thereby optimizing patient outcomes across multiple specialties.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is classified as a malignant tumor in the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان), indicating that this metastatic cancer represents a specific form of malignant neoplasm affecting the sublingual salivary gland. Recognizing this relationship clarifies the pathological nature and precise anatomical location of the disease, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning in patients with salivary gland malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 277
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on Nose are associated with nasal disorder (اِضطِراب أنفي).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is classified as a dental procedure, indicating that it has a relationship with the broader category of dental procedures. This connection underscores that the act of extracting a single tooth is a specific type of dental intervention within the overall field of dental procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1173
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures on Nose are associated with اِضطِراب أنفي, indicating that these interventions address nasal disorders. Within this broader category, control of epistaxis is identified as a specific type of procedure (inverse_isa) that falls under Procedures on Nose. The control of epistaxis procedure is performed directly on the nos, meaning it targets the nose itself. Together, these relationships illustrate that managing nosebleeds is a direct, targeted intervention on the nose and forms part of the wider set of procedures used to treat nasal disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 225
retrieved_docs.content: Extraction of single tooth is a direct procedure performed on zuby (teeth), indicating that the teeth are the specific anatomical site for this dental intervention. Disorders of teeth and jaw are described as having zuby as their parent category, meaning that conditions affecting the teeth and jaw are organized under the broader classification of zuby. Consequently, zuby functions both as the procedural target for single‑tooth extraction and as the foundational element within the hierarchy of dental and jaw disorders, underscoring its central importance in dental treatment and pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2146
retrieved_docs.content: The larynx serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system, indicating that certain respiratory interventions that are not directly therapeutic to the larynx can nonetheless involve the larynx indirectly. This relationship highlights the importance of recognizing the larynx’s involvement when planning and performing non‑therapeutic respiratory procedures, as indirect effects on the larynx may impact airway function and patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2878
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on the nose, mouth, and pharynx are classified as a subcategory of عمليات الأنف, indicating that these examinations are considered part of the broader group of nasal operations.
